next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

Lin was awake before the tram started screaming down the hill.

They sat at the table in Gabe's old dock-union shirt, the green one with the cracked white gull on the pocket, sorting used receipts by size. The neat stack on the left was for grocery paper, the soft stack on the right for the glossy warranty slips CommonFrame mailed to renters after every disputed fee. Lin liked the glossy ones because the pen skated. They had written their name three times in block capitals and once in a slanted hand that belonged to somebody older, somebody who had once filled out school forms in a hurry.

"Do offices always smell cold?" they asked without looking up.

Mira had one shoe on and her lunch jammed into the side pocket of her work bag. "This one does."

Lin considered that seriously, then wrote COLD on the back of a tram notice and underlined it twice as if it might help.

Gabe came out of the bedroom dragging his left foot a little, brace still open under his T-shirt, hair pressed flat on one side. He had a spoon in his mouth and a bowl of dry cereal in his hand because milk cost more than cereal did this week. "You going in early or hiding from us?"

"Audit."

"That's worse."

Gabe was right. The audit had started as an ugly rumor on Friday, passed desk to desk with the same flat voice people used for mold, layoffs, and surprise fee reviews. People whispered about missing disposal paperwork, a return packet that did not reconcile, and someone from compliance staying late. Yesterday an all-staff notice had come through with three passive verbs and no names. Records...
open_hooks_to_carry: Build a genuine quiet chapter around food, clothes, and a room being made livable.
Let Lin's memory fragments reveal classed, ordinary human lives rather than exotic spectacle.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d over his mouth. "I think somebody built you out of corners that were never meant to meet. That's not the same thing."

Lin absorbed that. "All right."

Mira had one hand on the door. The alley heat pushed in around her wrist. She looked back at Tavi, at the wrecked sheet, the torn line, the blanked monitor, the room where the whole book of her life had just lost one of its oldest nouns.

"I'll get them out before dawn," she said.

"They're already out," he said.

It was not reassurance. It was worse and truer than that.

Mira put her palm lightly between Lin's shoulder blades and led them into the alley. They moved with caution but not helplessness, one hand holding the oversize coat closed, the other brushing the brick wall once as if to be sure it was solid. Behind them Tavi locked the service door, one bolt and then the other.

The walk uphill to Brine Court would take twelve minutes if Lin could manage the grade and the shoes did not betray them. Twelve minutes through canal-market runoff, through the smell of fried fish and coolant, through a city that had room for leased bodies, pulped bodies, damaged bodies, debtor bodies, sex bodies, labor bodies, but none at all for the fact of one deciding to stay alive.

Mira spent the first block pretending that made the decision practical instead of permanent.

Lin kept close enough that their sleeve touched Mira's elbow each time they turned a corner.

By the top of the block she was no longer moving stolen stock through the dark. She was smuggling a person home, and every step after that belonged to a new category of crime.
state_deltas_required: Mira can still tell herself Lin is temporary evidence, but daily life makes that story harder to maintain with every ordinary act.
Mira commits to protecting Lin as someone with a future, not merely as proof against CommonFrame.
